---
title: "Prospector Setup"
author: "Tinashe M. Tapera"
format: gfm
---

To help with our setup of pipelines and projects,
we're creating this setup notebook to track how
we build environments. There are a number of goals we
want to achieve:

- Reproducible scientific environment and toolkit at all costs
- "One-button" setup (this notebook tracks this)
- Comfortable working ergonomics (IDEs, etc.)

To balance these needs, we're going to use this notebook
to reproducibly set the different components of the
build process. Currently, we are learning how to use
Singularity to build containers, and installing all of
our toolkit and IDE in that container.

## The Singularity file

By rendering this file, any code blocks get written to the
file in `{output-dir}/{output-name}`. This means we can write
a singularity definition file in this notebook without
too much hassle! We'll define these as Bash code chunks,
but not render any of them —  hopefully this will avoid
any confusion about rendering and executing any shell
chunks.

### Spack Base

We first want to define the tools necessary for the work itself;
the scientific substrate, according to ChatGPT. This should _always_
be written down. We use Spack for this and it is fairly easy
to add the packages with `spack add`. Importantly, for this, we
also add a `container: format: singularity` field to the `spack.yaml`
file. To see what this looks like, we can use the Singularity Python tool,
which was added to our `uv` virtual environment.

```{python}
from pathlib import Path
from pyprojroot import here
from spython.main.parse.parsers import SingularityParser
from spython.main.parse.writers import SingularityWriter

spack_containerize_output = here() / ".devcontainer/spack_recipe.def"
final_def_file = here() / ".devcontainer/prospectorDHSNepal.def"

parser = SingularityParser(spack_containerize_output)
recipe = parser.recipe
recipe.keys()
```

In general, we can see that the `singularity-python` API works well
for an object oriented interface:

```{python}
final = recipe['final']
```

We can see that despite this OO interface, the objects themselves
are pretty easy to understand; they're just chunks of a
singularity definition:

```{python}
final.install
```

So we can go ahead and add our sections:

## Build

We have to modify build somewhat for `pkg-config` to work:

```{python}
build = recipe["build"]

for i, block in enumerate(build.install):
    if "spack -e . install" in block:
        build.install[i] = block.replace(
            "spack -e . install",
            """spack -e . install pkgconf openssl
spack -e . load pkgconf openssl

export PKG_CONFIG="$(command -v pkg-config)"
export OPENSSL_DIR="$(spack -e . location -i openssl)"
export PKG_CONFIG_PATH="$OPENSSL_DIR/lib/pkgconfig:$OPENSSL_DIR/lib64/pkgconfig:${PKG_CONFIG_PATH:-}"

pkg-config --version
pkg-config --libs --cflags openssl

spack -e . install --fail-fast -j8"""
        )
        break
else:
    raise ValueError("Could not find `spack -e . install` in build.install")
```

### Linux Libraries

We'll add pandoc, curl, gdebi, and create a working directory:

```{python}
final.install.append("""
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
  pandoc \
  pandoc-citeproc \
  curl \
  gdebi-core \
  ca-certificates \
  && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

mkdir -p /work
""")
```

### Environment Vars

Some environment variables will be added, so that the newly
installed packages are available in the path. We'll also config some
stuff for GDAL and PROJ:

```{python}
final.environ.extend([
    'export PATH="/home/vscode/.local/bin:/home/vscode/.cargo/bin:/opt/views/view/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin"',
    'export R_HOME="/opt/views/view/rlib/R"',
    'export R_LIBS_USER="/work/rv/library"',
    'export PROJ_LIB="/opt/views/view/share/proj"',
    'export GDAL_DATA="/opt/views/view/share/gdal"',
    'export PKG_CONFIG_PATH="/opt/views/view/lib/pkgconfig:/opt/views/view/lib64/pkgconfig:/opt/views/view/share/pkgconfig"',
])
```

### Files

The files entry can be used to fix some structurally necessary files:

```{python}
final.files = [
    ["/opt/spack-environment", "/opt/spack-environment"],
    ["/opt/software", "/opt/software"],
    ["/opt/views", "/opt/views"],
]
```

### Quarto

Quarto is foundational to our notebook drive workflow, so we add it explicitly:

```{python}
final.install.append(r"""
curl -LO https://quarto.org/download/latest/quarto-linux-amd64.deb
gdebi --non-interactive quarto-linux-amd64.deb
quarto check
rm -f quarto-linux-amd64.deb
""")
```

### `arf` (Another R Frontend

`arf` is currently my favourite way to write R code; it's basically a nicer R terminal:

```{python}
final.install.append(r"""
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -LsSf \
  https://github.com/eitsupi/arf/releases/latest/download/arf-console-installer.sh | sh
ARF_BIN="$(command -v arf || true)"
if [ -z "$ARF_BIN" ]; then
  ARF_BIN="$(find /root /usr/local /opt -type f -name arf 2>/dev/null | head -n 1)"
fi
test -n "$ARF_BIN"
install -m 0755 "$ARF_BIN" /usr/local/bin/arf
/usr/local/bin/arf --version
""")
```

### `rv` R Project Package Manager

Though we use `spack` to install R and the different packages' dependencies,
the actual management of the R stack is handled by [`rv`](https://a2-ai.github.io/rv-docs/).

```{python}
final.install.append(r"""
curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/A2-ai/rv/refs/heads/main/scripts/install.sh | bash
RV_BIN="$(command -v rv || true)"
if [ -z "$RV_BIN" ]; then
  RV_BIN="$(find /root /usr/local /opt -type f -name rv 2>/dev/null | head -n 1)"
fi
test -n "$RV_BIN"
install -m 0755 "$RV_BIN" /usr/local/bin/rv
rv --version
""")
```

### User Health Check

We also add a line to define the internal user and check the functionality before closing out:

```{python}
final.install.append(r"""
id -u vscode >/dev/null 2>&1 || useradd -m -s /bin/bash vscode

command -v code-server && code-server --version
command -v arf && arf --version
command -v quarto && quarto check
command -v R
""")
```

## Write

Now, we can simply write this file out:

```{python}
writer = SingularityWriter(recipe)
out = writer.convert(runscript='/entrypoint.sh /bin/bash "$@"', force=True)

final_def_file.write_text(out)
```

We notice, however, that the output file has an issue in the `files from build`
section:

```
  60 │ %files
  61 │ /opt/spack-environment /opt/spack-environment
  62 │ /opt/software /opt/software
  63 │ /opt/views /opt/views
  64 │ %files from build
  65 │ / o p t / s p a c k - e n v i r o n m e n t   / o p t
  66 │ / o p t / s o f t w a r e   / o p t
  67 │ / o p t / v i e w s   / o p t
  68 │ / o p t / s p a c k - e n v i r o n m e n t / e n v i r o n m e n t _ m o d i f i c a t i o n s . s h   / o p
     │ t / s p a c k - e n v i r o n m e n t / e n v i r o n m e n t _ m o d i f i c a t i o n s . s h
  69 │ %post
  70 │ # Symlink the old view location
```

To fix this, some quick Python:

```{python}
from pathlib import Path
import string

text = final_def_file.read_text()

remove_ws = str.maketrans("", "", string.whitespace)

fixed_lines = []
in_files = False

for line in text.splitlines():
    stripped = line.strip()

    if stripped.startswith("%files"):
        in_files = True
        fixed_lines.append(line)
        continue

    if in_files and stripped.startswith("%"):
        in_files = False
        fixed_lines.append(line)
        continue

    if in_files and stripped:
        compact = line.translate(remove_ws)

        # Restore the source/destination separator for known copied paths.
        if compact.startswith("/opt/spack-environment/opt"):
            line = "/opt/spack-environment /opt"
        elif compact.startswith("/opt/software/opt"):
            line = "/opt/software /opt"
        elif compact.startswith("/opt/views/opt"):
            line = "/opt/views /opt"
        elif compact.startswith("/opt/spack-environment/environment_modifications.sh/opt/spack-environment/environment_modifications.sh"):
            line = "/opt/spack-environment/environment_modifications.sh /opt/spack-environment/environment_modifications.sh"
        else:
            line = compact

    fixed_lines.append(line)

final_def_file.write_text("\n".join(fixed_lines) + "\n")
```

And with that, we're done!
